Victor, you have five minutes before the board. Give me the idea, and tell me why I should fund it.
Smart self-service kiosks in our fifty busiest branches. The value proposition is simple: faster service for clients, and our teams freed for the work that actually grows revenue.
Nice in theory. But what would it actually do to our numbers?
If we placed the kiosks in those branches, we would cut transaction time in half. The bottom line is a return inside eighteen months, and the model is fully scalable.
Two million is a lot of conviction for a maybe. Why now?
The momentum is already here. Three branch managers asked for exactly this. If we moved now, we would lead the market instead of chasing it.
The board will say it is too risky for a full launch.
I would argue the opposite. If we ran a two-branch pilot first, we would learn fast and lower the risk before any rollout. The ask is small; the upside is not.
The value proposition is clear: if we automated the routine work, we would win back hundreds of hours a month.
If we tied a clear incentive to the pilot, we would earn the branch managers buy-in in a week.
The value proposition is simple: faster service at half the cost.
If we placed the kiosks, we would cut transaction time in half.
If we delayed this, we would hand the advantage to a competitor.
The ask is small: a two-branch pilot, scalable on success.

Good morning, and thank you for the time. I want to put one idea on the table today: a regional self-service kiosk for our LATAM clients. Here is the value proposition. If we placed smart kiosks in our fifty busiest branches, we would cut transaction time in half and free up our staff for higher-value work. Picture this. A client walks in, completes a routine deposit in ninety seconds, and walks out, while our team focuses on the relationships that actually grow revenue. The bottom line is simple. If we invested two million now, we would see a return inside eighteen months, and the model is fully scalable to other regions. I would argue that this is not just a cost saving. It is a genuine game changer for how we serve the market. The momentum is already there. Three branch managers have asked for exactly this. So the real question is not whether we can afford to do it. It is whether we can afford to wait. I would appreciate your support to run a pilot in two branches this quarter.

Hi Victor, it is Sophie. I listened to your draft pitch and I have a few quick thoughts before the board meeting. First, your hook is strong, so lead with it. Open with the second conditional, not with background. If we placed kiosks in the busiest branches, we would cut wait times in half. That sentence does the work of three slides. Second, every claim needs a tangible number. The board does not buy adjectives, they buy evidence, so pair every benefit with a figure. Third, name the upside and the rationale in the same breath. If we ran the pilot, we would learn fast and we would lower the risk of a full rollout. And one more thing. When they push back on the cost, do not get defensive. Reframe it. If we delayed this, we would simply hand the advantage to a competitor. Keep the energy high and the ask small, a two-branch pilot, not the whole region. Land that and the rest follows. You have a real game changer here, so go and sell it. Let me know how it goes.
